See Illustrated Flora of B.C. note "The three Agropyron species occurring in BC 
hybridize readily when growing together, forming morphologically 
intermediate plants. In addition, some cultivars are also intermediate, 
being derived from hybrids. Consequently, identification of Agropyron 
species in North America is often extremely difficult. In many 
instances, it may be best to refer to plants of this genus as A. 
cristatum sens. lat., the last phrase indicating no distinction is being
made between the three species." Critical comment by ? GBIF Occurrence record includes subtaxon: A. cristatum subsp. pectinatum (TL)

critical_comments: Originally labelled as B. pumila. Annotated to B. hallii by Maas, 1993. Betula pumila L. var. glandulifera Regel = Betula pumila L. Note: This species apparently hybridizes with B. pendula and B. papyrifera in our area (Brayshaw 1996b). Source: The Illustrated Flora of British Columbia (TL)
OriginalSaanichComment: Margins of marshes; lakes and peat bogs; Rithet's Bog and Lost Lake. In the past, it has been customary to recognize this species either as a separate species, B. hallii Howell, or as a variety of the eastern B. pumila, or as a variety of B. glandulosa. This interesting species requires more critical study to solve the relationship between the various taxa involved, and for the time being the authors prefer to use the more customary name of B. pumila var. glandulifera. (B. glandulosa var. hallii Howell) Some exotic species of birches can be found occasionally growing in Royal Oak and a few other areas; Eurasian Silver Birch (Betula verrucosa Ehrh.) and European Birch (Betula pubescens Ehrh.) are the most common among them.

(AS); A. patula is a Eurasian weed and probably based on old taxonomy.  I would not trust it without a verifying specimen; Atriplex dioica is in for sure. critical_comments: Combination C. gmelinii (Bray) Steud. of 1840 is a synonym of the Asian C. tataricum.  C. gmeliniii J.M. Coult. & Rose of 1900  is an illegitimate name because of the publication of the 1840 name.  The latter name is referential to our C. pacificum. (DG); while both Bray and Chamisso & Schlechtendal thought they were describing the same plant as Gmelin's pre-Linnaean phrase name, neither were... Bray's plant is from Latvia, C&S's is from the Bering coast of Alaska, and Gmelin's "in omni Sibiria crescens". (C&S's name is also illegitimate, for a separate reason than Coulter & Rose's.) Interestingly, the most recent (2003) revision of Conioselinum does (as David mentioned) dramatically expand C. tataricum from central Asia to include both Bray's and Gmelin's concept. That paper also calls our plants C. chinense, but as they really only focus on Eurasia they appear to be parroting Mathias and Constance's treatment in the vol 28 of the North American Flora (1944-1945). Cronquist's criticisms (Vasc Plants PNW vol 3, 1961) appear completely justified, and the M&C measurements appear to be based almost exclusively on Pacific material (their seed size figures exclude those for New England material given by Cronquist). The M&C treatment was prepared while Mathias was still at Berkeley with Constance, and it's entirely possible that they had insufficient material of C. chinense sensu stricto from New England. The 2003 paper does not cite Cronquist.
